extra.reasoning:
This is a state machine with variables opted_out (bool), sent (bool), ticks (int in [0,12]); initial state opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0. The safety property requires that globally, opted_out implies not sent (whenever opted_out is true, sent must be false).

Most transitions preserve the property: t1 (optout) sets opted_out=true AND sent=false; t3 (send_attempt when opted_out) sets sent=false; t4 (tick) sets sent=false and increments ticks capped at 12; t2 (send_attempt when not opted_out) sets sent=true but only while opted_out is false (no violation).

The flaw is t5: on send_attempt, if (opted_out AND ticks>=12), it sets sent:=true. This sets sent=true while opted_out is true, which directly violates the invariant. The state (opted_out=true, ticks>=12) is reachable, and when send_attempt fires there, t5 is enabled (its guard holds), so a valid execution can take it.

Counterexample (14 events, within the 16-event bound): fire tick 12 times to drive ticks from 0 to 12 (capped at 12); these keep opted_out=false, sent=false. Then fire optout (t1): opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=12. Then fire send_attempt: both t3 (guard: opted_out) and t5 (guard: opted_out and ticks>=12) are enabled; choosing t5 sets sent:=true. The resulting state has opted_out=true AND sent=true, so opted_out implies not sent is false. Because a safety property must hold on every reachable execution and this execution is reachable, the property is violated.
